title: Tunisia vs Japan World Cup 2026 Predictions: Back Japan
original_text:
Tunisia sit bottom of Group F with zero points and a goal difference of -4 after their opening 5-1 defeat to Sweden, meaning a loss here would almost certainly end their World Cup 2026 campaign before it has truly begun. Japan, meanwhile, picked up a point from a 2-2 draw with Netherlands and know that three points in this fixture would put them in a commanding position to reach the knockout rounds, potentially leapfrogging Sweden at the top if results elsewhere go their way. For Tunisia, this is effectively a must-win match; for Japan, it is a genuine chance to take control of Group F.

Japan are the standout pick at 8/15 in a fixture where Tunisia are reeling from a heavy defeat and lack the cutting edge to trouble a well-organised Samurai Blue side. Back Japan to win and over 2.5 goals at 6/5 as a combined read, given the gulf in quality on display in both sides’ opening matches.

This Tunisia vs Japan prediction hinges heavily on what we saw in each side’s opening fixture. Tunisia were dismantled by Sweden in their first World Cup 2026 match, conceding five and looking desperately short of defensive organisation at the highest level. Ironically, their CAF qualifying campaign had been impeccable — six wins, sixteen goals scored, and not a single goal conceded — but those opponents were a far cry from Sweden, and now Japan, in their group.

Japan, on the other hand, showed real resilience against Netherlands, coming back twice to earn a 2-2 draw. Daichi Kamada and Keito Nakamura both scored on Matchday 1, and Hajime Moriyasu’s side demonstrated the kind of composure and tactical flexibility that has characterised their recent World Cup campaigns. With Takefusa Kubo pulling strings, Japan carry genuine creative threat at both ends of the pitch.

The narrative is stark. Tunisia need goals badly and must attack, yet that openness could simply gift Japan the space their quick transitions thrive on. Moriyasu’s side have the quality to punish a depleted and demoralised Tunisian unit, and at 8/15, the Tunisia vs Japan odds accurately reflect that reality.

Tunisia’s pre-tournament preparation offered little cause for optimism, with three defeats and a goalless draw across their friendlies. That run came against Belgium and Austria — hardly the most formidable opponents at this level — and the 5-1 opener against Sweden confirmed the fear that Tunisia’s defensive solidity in CAF qualifying simply did not translate against elite European opposition. They have scored just two goals across their last four competitive and friendly outings combined.

Japan arrive in Monterrey on the back of a genuinely impressive run of form. That pre-tournament sequence included a win over England away from home — a result that underlined their credentials as more than just a group-stage participant. Their 2-2 draw with Netherlands on Matchday 1 showed they can be breached, but also that they have the attacking resources to find a way back into matches at the very highest level.

The Tunisia vs Japan head to head record leans decisively in Japan’s favour across five meetings. Japan have won four of those five encounters and have never lost to Tunisia in a competitive fixture. The most significant result came at the 2002 World Cup, when Japan beat Tunisia 2-0 in the group stage — a result that resonates given the similar context of this Matchday 10 meeting.

Tunisia’s only win in this series came in the 2022 Kirin Cup, when they beat Japan 3-0 — a result that serves as a reminder that upsets are possible, but that came in a pre-tournament friendly context. In the four fixtures classed as competitive or at major tournaments, Japan are unbeaten, and that historical edge supports the current Tunisia vs Japan betting odds pointing firmly toward a Japan victory.

Tunisia were led into this tournament by Sabri Lamouchi, appointed in early 2026 following a managerial change. The Tunisian squad is not without experienced names: Ellyes Skhiri, who captains the side, has 83 caps and brings leadership from his Eintracht Frankfurt base, while Hannibal Mejbri (Burnley) and Anis Ben Slimane (Norwich City) give the midfield some European-league pedigree. The big concern after that Sweden defeat is defensive organisation, with Omar Rekik, on just six caps, having scored Tunisia’s consolation goal but the backline looking thoroughly exposed at high speed.

Japan carry one of the most internationally seasoned squads in their history. Yuto Nagatomo, at 39 years old with 145 caps, remains available as an experienced option in defence. Daichi Kamada (Crystal Palace) and Ritsu Doan (Eintracht Frankfurt) provide creativity in behind the striker, and Ayase Ueda of Feyenoord leads the attack with 16 international goals to his name. There are no significant injury concerns reported for Moriyasu’s squad heading into this fixture.

Tunisia’s squad depth is a question mark. Several of their attacking players, including Elias Achouri (Copenhagen) and Elias Saad (Hannover 96), will need to step up if the side is to avoid a second successive heavy defeat. The hosts will also need a much-improved display from goalkeeper Aymen Dahmen, who was exposed badly against Sweden’s pace and directness.

Predicted lineups based on available squad information — squads to be confirmed closer to kick-off.

The central duel to watch is Ellyes Skhiri against Japan’s midfield, though the dynamic has shifted significantly after the late withdrawal of captain Wataru Endo through injury. Skhiri carries Tunisia’s entire midfield burden: 83 caps of experience, Bundesliga know-how from Eintracht Frankfurt, and a remit to break play up and drive forward. With Endo ruled out, Ao Tanaka shoulders greater responsibility as Japan’s midfield anchor — the Leeds United man has the combative instincts and defensive discipline, but the loss of 73-cap experience alongside him is a genuine blow to Japan’s screening unit. If Skhiri can exploit that reduced cover and connect defence to attack, Tunisia can stay in the contest. Japan’s transition game flourishes when the opposition midfield is disrupted, and Takefusa Kubo’s movement and Daichi Kamada’s runs from deep are calibrated to exploit exactly that kind of space — but generating the ball recoveries to trigger those transitions without Endo’s engine will be Moriyasu’s key challenge.

Japan to win @ 8/15. The Tunisia vs Japan picks landscape is fairly straightforward once you strip back the noise. Japan are unbeaten in their last four competitive meetings with Tunisia and arrive in Monterrey with momentum, depth, and a manager who has navigated knockout football at multiple World Cups. Tunisia have conceded ten goals across their last three matches including pre-tournament friendlies, and their defensive fragility against pacy attacking play is a serious structural problem Japan will target.

Over 2.5 goals @ 6/5. Both sides’ opening fixtures at this World Cup produced four goals apiece. Tunisia conceded five against Sweden, Japan exchanged two goals apiece with Netherlands. The Tunisia vs Japan best bets case for goals rests on Tunisia’s defensive frailty at this level and Japan’s willingness to press high and create in volume. The 6/5 available on over 2.5 looks generous given the evidence.

Daichi Kamada anytime scorer (recommended: check leading operators for best available price). Kamada already has a goal in this tournament from the Netherlands draw, and his recent scoring record across the last cycle makes him one of the more reliable attacking threats in this Japan squad. With Tunisia likely to be stretched and commit players forward out of desperation, the space in behind is exactly the kind of situation in which the Crystal Palace midfielder thrives.

For those building a Tunisia vs Japan accumulator or bet builder, consider combining Japan to win with over 2.5 goals. Japan have not lost to Tunisia in a competitive fixture and arrive in far superior form. Adding Kamada as an anytime scorer to that acca or as a standalone Tunisia vs Japan bet builder leg strengthens the value case further. This match also fits as a Tunisia vs Japan each way consideration in the group-winner market, where Japan at 55/1 for the tournament represent genuine each-way potential for accumulator builders.

Prices sourced across leading operators and correct at time of publication. Always compare across the best available options before placing.

Tunisia vs Japan is live in the UK on BBC and available to stream free via BBC iPlayer. Kick-off is at 04:00 BST on Sunday, 22 June 2026 (22:00 local time in Monterrey, UTC-6). The match takes place at Estadio BBVA in Guadalupe, Monterrey, Mexico, which is hosting several World Cup 2026 group-stage fixtures across the tournament.

That early experience shaped a lifelong devotion to the club and a genuine curiosity about the stories, characters, and moments that have defined Celtic across the decades.
